source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 5.4 psmisc & 5.5 watch (pp. 76-77)

questions.q: Display the running processes as a tree structure (the psmisc command for this).
questions.why: pstree shows the current processes arranged as a tree, making parent/child relationships visible.

questions.q: Send a kill signal to every process named httpd, by name.
questions.why: killall sends a signal to all processes identified by the given name (unlike kill, which takes a PID).

questions.q: What does the fuser command identify?
options: The PIDs of the processes that are using specified files or file systems
The free space on a file system
questions.why: fuser reports which processes (by PID) are using a given file or file system.

questions.q: Kill the processes that are accessing the file /etc/httpd/conf/httpd.conf, using fuser.
questions.why: fuser -k sends the kill signal to every process using the named file.

questions.q: What does the watch command do?
options: Runs a command repeatedly at intervals and shows its output full-screen
Monitors a file for permission changes
Records a terminal session to a file
Watches a process until it exits, then reports
questions.why: watch re-runs a command every few seconds and displays the latest result in the terminal.
-n sets the number of seconds between executions; here it repeats the tail command every 5 seconds.

options: Press Ctrl+C to kill the process
Wait for it to finish on its own
questions.why: watch runs until interrupted; Ctrl+C kills it and returns you to the prompt.
